---
title: "MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging"
subtitle: "From inverse problems to neural networks"
---

MATH 435 studies images through one central question:

> When an imaging system gives us incomplete, blurred, noisy, or transformed data, how can we reconstruct the image, and how do we know whether the reconstruction should be trusted?

The course begins with classical mathematical imaging: images as arrays and functions, convolution, Fourier analysis, noise, ill-posedness, regularization, variational models, sparsity, and wavelets. These ideas naturally lead to modern neural reconstruction methods. In this course, neural networks are not introduced as magic black boxes. They appear as learned inverse maps, learned priors, learned denoisers, or learned components inside reconstruction algorithms.

The guiding progression is:

$$
y = Ax+\eta
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\hat{x}
=
\arg\min_x D(Ax,y)+\lambda R(x)
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\hat{x}=f_\theta(y)
$$

and to hybrid methods such as plug-and-play reconstruction, where the forward model is still used but part of the prior knowledge is learned from data.

## How To Use This Site

The [syllabus](syllabus.qmd) is the authority for course grading, assessment dates, and course policies. The other pages support teaching, practice, notebooks, and project preparation without adding separate graded work.

| Resource | Use it for |
|---|---|
| [Class roadmap and slides](classes.qmd) | Find the material for each class meeting. |
| [How to prepare for class](preparation.qmd) | Use the class rhythm connecting book, slides, notebooks, practice, quizzes, and projects. |
| [Ungraded practice](practice.qmd) | Use optional practice prompts connected to the notebooks. |
| [Quiz information](quizzes.qmd) | Check quiz timing, allowed tools, and preparation guidance. |
| [Notebooks](notebooks/index.qmd) | Run and modify the computational experiments, including Colab links. |
| [Online book](book/index.qmd) | Read the motivation chapter and fourteen narrative chapters. |
| [Syllabus](syllabus.qmd) | Check course policies, schedule, and grading. |
| [Final project](project/index.qmd) | Understand the LLM-aware project, rubric, and oral defense. |
| [LLM-use statement](llm-use.qmd) | Read how Codex assisted development and how instructor responsibility was maintained. |

## Course Arc

| Part | Weeks | Main Question |
|---|---:|---|
| Foundations | 1-3 | What is an image mathematically, and how do imaging systems transform it? |
| Inverse problems | 4-6 | What makes reconstruction unstable, and how does regularization help? |
| Variational methods | 7-9 | How do we turn modeling assumptions into optimization problems? |
| Sparse and learned priors | 10-13 | How do representations, data, and denoisers encode prior knowledge? |
| Reliability and projects | 14, then Week 15 defense | When should a reconstruction be trusted, and how do students defend their work? |

## Central Idea

Classical imaging asks:

> What assumptions should we impose on the unknown image?

Neural imaging asks:

> Can those assumptions, or the reconstruction algorithm itself, be learned from examples?

The course treats both questions as part of the same mathematical story. Every method, classical or learned, must still be judged by its forward model, its assumptions, its stability, its failures, and its consequences.
